answer: The built-in iter() function takes an arbitrary object and tries to return an iterator that will return the object’s contents or elements, raising TypeError if the object doesn’t support iteration.

Generator expressions return an iterator that computes the values as necessary, not needing to materialize all the values at once, while list comprehensions return a list containing the resulting values, materializing all values at once. Generator expressions are preferable when working with infinite streams or very large data, and are written with parentheses, while list comprehensions use square brackets.

The `yield` keyword in a Python generator function outputs a value, suspends the function’s state of execution, and preserves local variables so that the function can be resumed later, returning an iterator that produces a stream of values.

The constructor for partial() takes the arguments (function, arg1, arg2, ..., kwarg1=value1, kwarg2=value2). The resulting object is callable, so you can just call it to invoke function with the filled-in arguments.
